title: Macro Definitions
text: Macro Definitions The translation table compiler supports up to ten macros, ^M0 through ^M9, for commonly-used combinations. The M can be uppercase or lowercase. For example, the following assignments map ^M0 to the PF1 key on a VT200 keyboard, and the combinations PF1 A and PF1 a to the Uniface ^ACCEPT function: ^escOP ^M0 PF1 7 bits mode ^M0^aA ^ACCEPT Copy Using another macro or the uppercase or lowercase function within a macro definition is not supported. The following macro definitions are not supported: ^M3^escP ^M5 INCORRECT nested macro ^esc^aA ^M4 INCORRECT upper/lowercase code Copy Note: You must define the macro before you use it. Several output sequences per macro Up to three different output sequences can be assigned to each macro. This capability is particularly useful when the keyboard can operate in several modes. The translation table compiler then generates a separate assignment for each sequence assigned to the macro. For example, a VT200 keyboard can operate in seven bits, eight bits, and eight bits with seven bits input. The input sequence (in the keyboard translation table) is different for each mode. The following macro definitions assign all three possible combinations for the PF1 key to macro ^M0. The combinations PF1 E and PF1 e are recognized as ^ERASE in all three keyboard modes. The lines generated are shown below: ^escOP ^M0 PF1 7 bits mode ^143P ^M0 PF1 8 bits mode ^015P ^M0 PF1 8 bits mode with 7 bit input ^M0^eE ^ERASE Copy This is equivalent to (but much more concise than): ^escOPe ^ERASE ^escOPE ^ERASE ^143Pe ^ERASE ^143PE ^ERASE ^015Pe ^ERASE ^015PE ^ERASE Copy If a source sequence contains more than one macro, each macro must be assigned the same number of combinations. CAUTION: The total number of bytes assigned to all macros together must not exceed 450 bytes. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
